text: Q1 leadership priorities: Alice owns Atlas launch (Postgres backend, ACID compliance), Bob owns Phoenix Kafka migration, Carol owns Horizon Beta, Dave owns Orion Alpha, Frank owns Titan Kubernetes. Eve coordinates product strategy and Nathan leads sales.

The Series A terms are signed. $18M to scale the Meridian platform. Key investments: 10 new engineering hires for Atlas, Phoenix, and Orion; enterprise sales team led by Nathan; data center expansion on GCP with Frank leading infra.

Alice has confirmed the Postgres decision for Atlas is final. This is a company-level architectural decision — our transaction ledger needs ACID guarantees and Postgres delivers this. MongoDB was rejected for eventual consistency issues.

Bob reports that the Phoenix Kafka migration is the single most impactful technical decision of Q1. Moving from RabbitMQ to Kafka enables the real-time event streaming that Atlas, Orion, and Horizon all depend on. The 600k msg/s headroom future-proofs us.

Q1 engineering decisions summary for the board: 1) Postgres for Atlas (ACID, Alice), 2) Kafka for Phoenix (scale, Bob), 3) TypeScript for Horizon (maintainability, Carol), 4) PyTorch for Orion (ML accuracy, Dave), 5) Kubernetes on GCP (ops, Frank).

The v2 pricing model is still undecided. Eve and Nathan are evaluating: seat-based vs usage-based vs hybrid. The Orion ML inference cost is the key variable — each PyTorch prediction costs real compute on GCP. TBD by end of Q2.

Frank has signed the GCP enterprise agreement. Committed spend saves 30% on our Kubernetes and Postgres compute costs. This significantly improves the Atlas unit economics. The GCP deal was approved by the board.

Nathan closed the first $500K ARR enterprise deal. The customer is specifically buying Atlas for its Postgres ACID compliance — they had a bad experience with MongoDB data inconsistency at their previous vendor. This validates our tech decisions.

Leadership OKR review: Q1 key results hit — Atlas to production (Alice), Phoenix Kafka migration (Bob), Horizon beta (Carol), Orion alpha (Dave), GCP infrastructure (Frank). Miss: Apex export feature pushed to Q2. Overall: strong Q1.

The decision to build Prism was approved by leadership. Morgan leads the roadmap, Alice owns the Postgres data model, Bob owns the Kafka streaming layer, Carol owns the Horizon dashboard integration. Q2 GA target is confirmed.

Alice's proposal for the Atlas v2 architecture is approved: Postgres primary for ACID transactions, read replica for reporting, Elasticsearch layer for full-text search, Redis cache for hot data. This will handle 100k TPS by Q3.

The Nexus API gateway project is greenlit. This is a major architectural decision: all external API traffic routes through Nexus (Nginx + JWT + OAuth). Frank owns infra, Alice defines the Atlas contract, Carol defines the Horizon contract.

Leadership agreed on the TypeScript standard for all frontend code at Meridian. Carol championed this after the pain of mixed JavaScript/TypeScript in the early Horizon codebase. No exceptions — even internal tools must use TypeScript.

The international expansion decision: We will expand to the EU in Q4. Frank is setting up GCP europe-west1 for Atlas Postgres data residency. Rachel is coordinating the GDPR compliance review. Nathan has three EU prospects ready.

Dave presented the Orion ML roadmap to leadership. Q2: GA with PyTorch prediction service on GCP. Q3: custom model fine-tuning for enterprise customers. Q4: integrate LangChain for natural language query across Atlas and Phoenix data.

Q2 budget allocation approved: 40% engineering (Atlas, Orion, Horizon v2), 30% sales and marketing (Nathan's team), 20% infrastructure (Frank's GCP), 10% G&A. Rachel is the budget owner for G&A. Frank owns the infrastructure budget.

The board has approved the v2 roadmap. Key milestones: Horizon v2 (Carol, Q2), Prism GA (Morgan, Q2), Orion GA (Dave, Q2), Nexus gateway (Frank, Q3), Vega analytics (Alice, Q3), international (Frank, Q4). Nathan has the commercial targets.

Leadership is aligned: the biggest risk to v2 launch is the Atlas Postgres scaling work. Alice needs three additional engineers for the connection pooling, replication, and Elasticsearch integration. Approved — posting the roles today.

Frank presented the Titan infrastructure roadmap to leadership. Key decision: stay on GCP vs multi-cloud. Leadership decided to commit to GCP as primary cloud — the GCP enterprise discount and Kubernetes GKE Autopilot are too valuable to split.

The Zenith customer success portal is approved for Q3 as a strategic investment. Morgan leads product, Carol leads frontend (React/TypeScript), Alice provides the Atlas data layer. This will reduce churn for enterprise Atlas customers.

Nathan's competitive analysis: our main competitors are using MongoDB — they've had publicized data consistency issues. Our choice of Postgres for Atlas is a major selling point with enterprise buyers who have been burned by eventual consistency.

The LangChain integration for Orion was discussed by leadership. Dave wants to use it for the natural language query feature. The question is: OpenAI API vs self-hosted models. Budget and data residency considerations. TBD — need Q3 decision.

Leadership has agreed: Meridian will open-source the Nexus API gateway and the Titan Kubernetes helm charts. This is a strategic decision to build developer community. Frank and Alice are preparing the GitHub repos. Target: Q3 release.

The Series B fundraise is being explored. Key metrics investors care about: Atlas transaction volume (Postgres), Horizon DAU (React/TypeScript), Orion prediction accuracy (PyTorch), Phoenix throughput (Kafka). Eve and Nathan preparing the deck.

Final Q1 decisions ratified by leadership: Postgres (Atlas, ACID), Kafka (Phoenix, scale), TypeScript (Horizon, maintainability), PyTorch (Orion, ML), Kubernetes on GCP (Titan, ops). These are the five foundational decisions of Meridian's technical stack.
